titleStmt.title: Letter from Wilfrid Spender to Lillian Spender, 15 November 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) to his wife, Lillian (1880-1968). Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Letters from Sir Wilfred Spender to Lady Spender, D1633/1/1/62

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
15 Nov My dear pr. Only time for a few lines as I
have been out all day with the bombers. On
my return I found Gen' Bols & Col Hudson waiting
to see 'me'. Both very friendly & as usual for
the time I at any rate ( & I think they also)
forgot the difference in our respective ranks, & had a nice buck. We shall stop here at x another week
& then remain in sight of our church spires
or towns. I won't say which for fear of giving
our position away! I wish I could be with you tomorrow but
know the p r will make a wise decision. I do not think the Comforts Commitee sounds much
of a success at present but if the p r takes it up know
it will get a better chance especially if Mrs B works
with her. On the other hand if you get a good
offer of work which will not take too much out
of you & is really helpful to the nation I shall
not be surprised if you take this. I won't advise
either way as it is unsafe to do so from this
distance. I like to see the various big doctors chasing
the p v !, but grow anxious sometimes lest they
use her for experimental purposes not to her own
good. I am more than ever convinced that my plan
is the right one for the Comforts Committee i e
to help the men to buy what they want & not
to give them presents. We are to get a plumpuddings
from the Daily News ! ! ! which has a fund for
the purpose for the army. This I cannot look upon
as disinterested on their part, & in any case I think
our men can eat them with a quiet conscience. No letter since the 10th which came
last night I am glad to get this, but look
forward to a bumper soon. By the way the
mail seems to be a very uncertain one so
you may expect big delays sometimes, even
if I am good in writing. As regards funds I find that my messbill has
every prospect of being by especially as it is won in
the porting system with £180 cook. I very much resent
this as I should like to feel I was doing all I could
to save for the loans. I wish our people would realise
this more, but the Govt don't put an example & the
people have no guides. Rubber has bounced up again I see & this should
materially increase our income this year, but the reason
is done to the sudden demand for rubber trench boots
which will not last after the war, & we must look
to a drop of another £1000 then. I sometimes wonder
if I am justified in not taking a chance of returning
to the army & its pay, but it does mean as
much humiliation in store in future that I do
not want to do it unless the little pr feels that
she could not manage if things got very bad
later. Our right course now is to try & help the
loans to our 0utmost extent, so that as soon as
the terms are mentioned I hope you will
send out the pass book to me , but it won't be
much use doing so till we know your future
life. If you wish to furnish a flat I am quite
agreeable to this, but may very possibly look on
that as a capital investment & sell out to
meet it. I do not want to lessen or loan
contributions, tho' there is no hurry about it.
The pinch won't be felt till next year, & I am
not sure it is not better for them who are willing
to subscribe , to hold back till then so that the screw
may now be put on the Govt. Best of love. Have to censorfor
the mind Wilfrid BSpender
